coredata.dc:title: Electric-field-tunable electronic properties of graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2009 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:issueName: 14th International Conference on Modulated Semiconductor Structures
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, R.B.
Chang, C.P.
Lin, M.F.
coredata.dc:description: The tight-binding method is employed to investigate the electronic properties of a square graphene quantum dot subject to an in-plane electric field (F). The electronic properties are strongly modified by tuning the field strength or altering the field direction. F will change state energies, alter energy gaps, and induce energy gap modulations. State energies show oscillatory behavior with the change of the field strength. The oscillating amplitude and period are further modulated by the change of the field direction. The field-orientation-dependent electronic properties originate in the geometrical anisotropy of the square graphene quantum dot. Moreover, the density of states (DOS), exhibiting many discrete peaks, directly reveals the characteristic of the electric-field-tunable electronic properties. The number and frequencies of DOS peaks are significantly dependent on the field strength and direction.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Electronic properties
Electric field

(a) The geometrical structure of (
N
a
= 13 ,
N
z
= 8 ) finite-sized graphene quantum dot. The angle θ represents the direction of the electric field.

The state energies vs. electric fields at θ =
0
∘
,
30
∘
,
60
∘
, and
90
∘
are shown in (a), (b), (c), and (d), respectively.
(a) Density of states for different F's at θ = 0 . (b) DOS for different θ 's at F = 0.07 .

head.ce:title: Electric-field-tunable electronic properties of graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of General Studies, National Kaohsiung Marine University, Kaohsiung, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for General Education, Tainan University of Technology, Tainan 710, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, National Cheng Kung University, Tainan 70101, Taiwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The tight-binding method is employed to investigate the electronic properties of a square graphene quantum dot subject to an in-plane electric field (F). The electronic properties are strongly modified by tuning the field strength or altering the field direction. F will change state energies, alter energy gaps, and induce energy gap modulations. State energies show oscillatory behavior with the change of the field strength. The oscillating amplitude and period are further modulated by the change of the field direction. The field-orientation-dependent electronic properties originate in the geometrical anisotropy of the square graphene quantum dot. Moreover, the density of states (DOS), exhibiting many discrete peaks, directly reveals the characteristic of the electric-field-tunable electronic properties. The number and frequencies of DOS peaks are significantly dependent on the field strength and direction.

ce:section.ce:para:
Graphene-based materials, such as three-dimensional (3D) graphite [1], two-dimensional (2D) ultrathin graphite films [2,3], 1D nanographite monoribbon [4,5], one-dimensional (1D) nanotubes [6], and 0D carbon toroids [7], are allotropes of graphene. Graphene is a planar hexagonal lattice made of carbon atoms. It is a zero gap semiconductor with two linear bands crossing at the Fermi energy (
E
F
= 0 ) . 3D graphite is the stack of the infinite number of graphene layers, coupled by the weak interlayer interactions. 3D graphite is a semimetal due to the tiny overlap between the conduction bands and valance bands. The recently fabricated 2D ultrathin graphite films are treated as the pile of the finite number of graphene layers. The low-energy electronic properties depend on the interlayer interactions, number of the layer and stacking types. A 1D armchair (zigzag) graphene ribbon is the strip of graphene with the parallel armchair (zigzag) edges. Such a 1D graphene ribbon elongates infinitely along one direction (this direction is typically assumed to coincide with the x-axis) while its width is finite along the y axis. The electronic properties of the 1D graphene ribbon are manipulated by controlling the geometric structure (ribbon width and ribbon edges).

A 1D carbon nanotube (CNT) is the wrapping of a graphene into a cylinder system. An infinite CNT can be classified as gapless metal, narrow-gap semiconductor, or moderate-gap semiconductor, depending on how it is rolled up from the graphene. Physical properties, magnetic properties, and optical properties of the nanotubes depend strongly on geometric structure (tube radius and charity). Furthermore, a quasi-one-dimensional CNT can be shortened by the scanning tunneling microscope (STM) and, thus, produce zero-dimensional finite CNTs. For example, a finite CNT with length
l
w
∼ 100
A
˚
is produced by the utility of a scanning tunneling microscope [8]. The reduction in size and dimensionality causes finite CNTs to exhibit very special physical properties, for example, electronic structure [9], optical properties [10], magnetic properties [11], and quantum transport properties [12]. The aforementioned graphitic systems exhibit outstanding physical properties, which are dominated by the dimensionality and geometric shape.

The influences of an electric field (F) on the electronic and optical properties of quasi-zero-dimensional finite carbon nanotubes are explored [13]. Electronic properties are not only significantly affected by the geometric structure (radius, length, and chirality) but also by the magnitude and direction of the electric field. By altering the geometrical symmetry of finite carbon nanotubes, the electric field can modify the electronic properties. Moreover, F influences the low-energy absorption spectra, exhibiting rich delta-function-like peaks, e.g., the change of frequency of the first peak, the change of the peak height, and the production of the new peaks.

A zero-dimensional graphene quantum dot is close to a zero-dimensional finite CNT. The former is seen as an unrolled zero-dimensional finite CNT. Thus, the electric field is also expected to have a great effect on the electronic properties of a zero-dimensional GQD. The single π -band tight-binding method is employed to intensely study the effect of electric field on the electronic properties (energy dispersions, edge states, subband spacing, energy gap, and DOS) of a GQD. The dependence of electronic properties on the electric field (field strength and field direction) is discussed. And this paper is organized as follows: The geometry of GQD and computational method are introduced in Section 2. Then, Section 3 discusses how the field strength and field orientation influence the electronic properties of a GQD. Finally, conclusion remarks are drawn in Section 4.

Low-energy electronic properties of zero-dimensional GQD subjected to an in-plane electric field are investigated. The geometrical structure of an (
N
a
,
N
z
) GQD is shown in Fig. 1 . All carbon atoms are packed in hexagonal lattice. The C–C bond length is b = 1.42
A
˚
. There are
N
a
and
N
z
carbon atoms along the x-axis and y-axis directions, respectively. That is, an (
N
a
,
N
z
) GQD is made up of
N
a
dimer lines and
N
z
zigzag lines. (
N
a
,
N
z
) is used to denote the characteristic of GQD. The total number of carbon atoms of GQD is N =
N
a
×
N
z
. The simple one-band tight binding method with the nearest neighbor interaction
γ
0
is adopted to fully simulate the low-energy properties of GQD.

Now, an electric field is applied to a GQD. The orientation of electric field is defined by θ , the angle between the electric field and the x axis. For simplicity, the rigid structure and the overlap integer (
γ
0
) of the GQD we consider here are presumed not to be affected by the electric field. Then, the screening effect, which is quite important for comprehending the electronic properties in the presence of external electric field, is essentially taken into consideration [5]. And the electrons of carbon atoms are then consequently going through the effective electric field. A weak field is expected to have a significant effect on the low-energy electronic properties. Therefore, only the
P
z
orbitals ( π bands) are actually introduced herein, and the σ bands (the C–H bonds) that are far apart from the Fermi energy are excluded. The Hamiltonian equation of the system is H | Φ ( x , y ) 〉 = E | Φ ( x , y ) 〉 . The tight-binding Bloch function could be expressed as
(1)
| Φ ( x , y ) 〉 =
∑
i = 1
N =
N
z
×
N
a
a
i
| φ (
R
i
) 〉 ,
where | φ (
R
i
) 〉 is the
P
z
orbitals at the
R
i
site and
a
i
is the site amplitude. The Hamiltonian operator is
(2)
H =
∑
i
ε
i
( F )
C
i
+
C
i
+
∑
i , j , i ≠ j
t
i , j
C
i
+
C
j
,
where i ( j ) denotes the atom sites.
ε
i
( F ) is the field-modulated site energy of the carbon atom.
C
i
+
(
C
i
) is the creation (annihilation) operator. The effect of effective electric field F is to add electric potential U = − e F ·
R
i
on the site energy of the π orbital of carbon atom, where F = ( F
cos
θ , F
sin
θ ) is the electric field and
R
i
= ( x , y ) is the coordinate of the carbon atom. The Hamiltonian matrix of GQD in the presence of effective electric field is a N × N Hermitian matrix. The Hamiltonian matrix elements, with the relation H ( i , j ) =
H
*
( j , i ) , are
(3)
H ( i , i ) = − e F ·
R
i
if
i = 1 , 2 , ⋯ , N ;
H ( i , j ) =
γ
0
if
|
R
i
−
R
j
| = b ;
H ( i , j ) = 0
others ,
where b = 1.42
A
˚
is the C–C bond length and
γ
0
= 2.598
eV is a hopping integral [5]. Energy dispersion
E
c , v
is obtained by diagonalizing the Hamiltonian matrix. The superscript c (v) represents the unoccupied
π
*
band (the occupied π band). The electronic properties varying with the field strength and direction will be exactly examined.

The energy spectra of a GQD are very sensitive to the geometrical structures and the magnitude of electric field [14]. In this work, we focus on electric-field-direction-dependent electronic properties of GQD. An (
N
a
= 13 ,
N
z
= 8 ) finite-sized graphene quantum dot is chosen as a model study. Such a dot is treated as a square dot because its length is almost equal to its width. Electric-field-tunable state energies are shown in Fig. 2 . The state energies, symmetrical about the Fermi energy (
E
f
= 0 ), away from
E
F
, are labeled by J = ± 1 , ± 2 , … . As shown, the state energies are tunable and controlled not only by the field strength but also by the field direction. At F = 0 , the state energies of the (
N
a
= 13 ,
N
z
= 8 ) GQD are discrete, which is similar to the discrete spectra of Electrons-in-a-Box. (
N
a
= 13 ,
N
z
= 8 ) GQD has a tiny gap
E
g
=
E
J = 1
−
E
J = − 1
= 0.0025
γ
0
. The wave function associated with J = 1 ( J = − 1 ) level is localized at the first and last zigzag lines. The electrons occupy cusps of the outmost zigzag lines. It is denoted as the edge state. After the turn-on of an electric field parallel to the x axis ( θ = 0 ),
E
c , v
oscillate with the field strength at low field regime. Notably, at F < 0.02
γ
0
/ e
A
˚
, the field has no significant effect on the state energy of | J | = 1 level. By contrast, at a relative high field, some high-energy states come to low-energy region and energy crossing happens. Moreover, at F ∼ 0.15
γ
0
/ e
A
˚
, some state energies are linearly dependent on the field strength because the electric potential due to the electric field dominates over the lattice potential.

The state energies strongly depend on the field direction. The case of θ =
90
∘
are shown in Fig. 2 (d). At F < 0.1
γ
0
/ e
A
˚
, the state energies are different from those of θ = 0 [Fig. 2(a)]. Such a difference originates in the anisotropy of crystal. The state energy of | J | = 1 level increases linearly with the field strength at F < 0.02
γ
0
/ e
A
˚
. The linear-F dependent characteristic results from the edge states. The electrons of the edge state, occupying cusps of the outmost zigzag lines, are almost isolated from the other electrons. Thus, the state energy is equal to its site energy ε . The electric-field-modulated site-energy of the carbon atoms located at the last zigzag line is ε = − eF ·
y
edge
, which is linearly dependent on the field strength. Therefore, the J = 1 level depicts the linear-F dependence. The state energies of | J | = 2 level oscillate with the field strength. QDT is a semiconductor at F < 0.15
γ
0
/ e
A
˚
due to the nonzero energy gap,
E
g
≠ 0 . The energy gap closes as the field strength grows. That is, semiconductor-metal-transition (SMT) occurs. As θ changes from
0
∘
to
90
∘
, the state energies exhibit θ -dependence [Figs. 2(b) and (c)]. At θ =
30
∘
, no linear-F dependent characteristic is found. The energy gap
E
g
opens and GQD is a semiconductor at F < 0.2
γ
0
/ e
A
˚
. In the case of θ =
60
∘
, at F < 0.02
γ
0
/ e
A
˚
, state energies of J = 1 level
E
J = 1
c , v
also show sublinear-F-dependent behavior. The main reason is that the site-energy of carbon atoms located at the
N
z
zigzag line is ε = − e ( F
cos
60
∘
·
y
edge
+ F
sin
60
∘
· x ) . The term − eF
cos
60
∘
·
y
edge
dominates the site-energy. In short, the state energies, energy gap, oscillation period, and oscillation strength depend on the field strength and the field direction.

The density of states (DOS) can reveal the electronic properties of GQD. DOS is defined as
(4)
D ( ω , F ) =
1
π
∑
i
N
Γ
[ ω −
E
i
( F )
]
2
+
Γ
2
,
where Γ =
10
− 3
γ
0
is the broadening parameter. The low-energy DOS, as shown in Fig. 3 , are symmetric about the Fermi level
E
F
. DOS exhibits many delta-function-like divergent peaks because of the discrete feature of the electronic states. The peak height is related to the state degeneracy and the peak position corresponds to the state energy. There is a doubly degenerate peak at
E
F
= 0 and several half-high peaks in the absence of F [Fig. 3(a)]. The application of an electric field parallel to the x axis will produce more peaks, shift peak positions, and alter peak heights [Fig. 3(a)]. DOS are symmetric about the Fermi level
E
F
. The energy gap, which is determined by the first peak, is opened by the electric field and metal-semiconductor-transition (MST) occurs. The size of
E
g
is modulated by the F. The inclusion of an electric field will alter the peak frequencies, create new peaks, open an energy gap, modulate the size of
E
g
, and induce SMT and MST. In addition to the field strength, DOS is considerable dependent on the field direction. DOS at F = 0.07
γ
0
/ e
A
˚
with different θ are shown in Fig. 3(b). Generally, DOS are symmetric about the Fermi level
E
F
. They exhibits many delta-function-like divergent peaks. The change of the field direction, from θ = 0 to θ =
90
∘
, will cause the shift of peaks, the change of the peak heights, and the alteration of the size of
E
g
.

Electric-field-tunable low-energy electronic properties of zero-dimensional finite-sized graphene quantum dot are investigated by employing the tight-binding model. The electric field will change state energies, alter energy gaps, and induce energy gap modulations. State energies oscillate significantly with the field strength. Their oscillating amplitude and period are modified by the alteration of field direction. State energies and energy spacings vary significantly with not only field strength but also field direction. Such a characteristic results from the spatial anisotropy of the graphene quantum dot. Moreover, the electric-field-tunable electronic properties will be directly revealed in DOS. The numbers and frequencies of peaks in DOS are strongly dependent on the field strength and direction.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Taiwan National Science Council (NSC 96-2112-M-165-001 MY3; NSC 97-2112-M-022-001-MY3).
